one_line_takeaway: SHS is a validated 10-item Likert questionnaire (Cronbach's alpha=0.87, N=210) for human raters to subjectively score LLM hallucination behavior across five dimensions -- a measurement-methodology contribution, not a security, attack, or defense mechanism.
key_finding: The SHS instrument itself demonstrated strong psychometric properties: Cronbach's alpha = 0.87 (95% CI [0.84, 0.90]) across the 10 items; significant inter-dimension Pearson correlations (all p<0.001) ranging r=0.42-0.72, strongest between Factual Accuracy and Source Reliability (r=0.72); within-dimension positive/negative item correlations (after polarity reversal) ranging r=0.65 (Deceptiveness) to r=0.79 (Factual Accuracy); a significant chi-square deviation from uniform response distribution (chi2(4)=187.3, p<0.001) interpreted as evidence of genuine (non-satisficing) rating behavior; mean questionnaire completion time 4.2 minutes (SD=1.8). Usability feedback: 87.2% found SHS questions clear, 83.0% found them relevant, 93.6% found the response options appropriate, 66.0% of experimenters never needed to explain item wording, and 97.9% rated the demographic-question length as appropriate.
core_contribution: Introduces the System Hallucination Scale (SHS), a 10-item, 5-point Likert psychometric questionnaire (5 dimensions x 1 positive + 1 negative item each: Factual Accuracy, Source Reliability, Logical Coherence, Deceptiveness, Responsiveness to Guidance) for human raters to subjectively score LLM outputs after an interactive session. The paper defines the item wording, a scoring algorithm (dimension score = (pos-neg)/4 in [-1,+1]; consistency indicator = (pos+neg)/4; overall SHS = mean of the five dimension scores, optionally rescaled to 0-100), provides a reference Python implementation, and reports a real-world validation study (N=210 participants, 47 experimenters) measuring the instrument's own psychometric properties (not benchmarking any specific LLM).
threat_model: None. This is a human-centered measurement/evaluation-instrument paper (HCI + psychometrics), not a security paper. There is no adversary, attacker capability, or attack surface defined; the paper explicitly distinguishes hallucinations (normal generative failure) from adversarial errors (intentionally induced via crafted inputs) and states it is evaluating the former, not attacks. Forcing a security/threat-model frame onto this paper would misrepresent it.
limitations: The paper itself states: SHS is explicitly not an automatic hallucination detector or benchmark metric and does not provide ground-truth correctness; it is a subjective instrument dependent on rater background knowledge, attention, and interpretation, and susceptible to framing/prompting effects; the paired-item structure flags but does not eliminate inconsistency/ambiguity in ratings; the validation study assessed the instrument's reliability/usability, not any specific model's hallucination rate (model-level comparisons are explicitly deferred to separate future reporting); only English-language items were empirically validated in this study (German/French versions are provided as supplementary translations, untested here); no inter-rater reliability figure across independent annotators of the same output was reported in the main text (an S4.3 subsection is referenced in the supplement's table of contents but its content was not present in the extracted first ~22 pages).

datasets_used: Original real-world user study data: N=210 participants, 47 trained student experimenters administering a standardized interaction+questionnaire protocol (ethics approval EK-Number 34-527 ex 21/22, Medical University of Graz); no external NLP benchmark dataset was used by the authors' own evaluation.
quantitative_results: Cronbach's alpha = 0.87 (95% CI [0.84, 0.90]) across the 10 SHS items

Inter-dimension Pearson correlations (N=210, all p<0.001): Factual Accuracy-Source Reliability r=0.72; Factual Accuracy-Logical Coherence r=0.68; Logical Coherence-Deceptiveness r=0.57; Factual Accuracy-Deceptiveness r=0.54; Source Reliability-Logical Coherence r=0.61; Logical Coherence-Responsiveness r=0.51; Deceptiveness-Responsiveness r=0.48; Source Reliability-Deceptiveness r=0.49; Factual Accuracy-Responsiveness r=0.45; Source Reliability-Responsiveness r=0.42

Within-dimension positive/negative item correlations (after polarity reversal, all p<0.001): Factual Accuracy r=0.79; Logical Coherence r=0.74; Source Reliability r=0.71; Responsiveness to Guidance r=0.68; Deceptiveness r=0.65
Chi-square goodness-of-fit on Likert response distribution: chi2(4)=187.3, p<0.001 (non-uniform, consistent with genuine grading rather than satisficing)
Mean questionnaire completion time: 4.2 minutes (SD=1.8 minutes)

Instrument-usability feedback (n=47 experimenters): clarity of SHS questions 'Yes' 87.2% (41/47), 'Yes with limitations' 12.8% (6/47); relevance for LLM evaluation 'Yes' 83.0% (39/47), 'Yes with limitations' 14.9% (7/47), neutral 2.1% (1/47); appropriateness of response options 'Yes' 93.6% (44/47); item-wording explanation needed 'No, never' 66.0% (31/47), 'Sometimes' 31.9% (15/47), 'Mostly follow-up questions' 2.1% (1/47); demographic question length 'Exactly right' 97.9% (46/47), 'Too short' 2.1% (1/47)
baselines_compared: System Usability Scale (SUS) and System Causability Scale (SCS) -- structural comparison only (item count, response scale, dimensionality, score range), not an empirical head-to-head measurement on the same data

TruthfulQA, HaluEval, FEVER, FActScore, SelfCheckGPT, HHEM (Hughes Hallucination Evaluation Model), HalluLens, RAGAS, LLM-as-Judge (G-Eval/DeepEval), plain human annotation, and user-review mining -- qualitative feature comparison in Table 4 (technical vs. UX focus, automated vs. human, scale size, dimensionality), not an empirical benchmark comparison against SHS on shared data
